externalUrls.title: Personal guidance for your athlete’s sport & year.

latestPosts.caption:
There are a lot of new faces here, so I wanted to properly introduce myself.

Before I ever coached, scouted, or recruited at the Division I level, I was an athlete trying to figure out recruiting too.

My family and I were trying to get seen, build the right profile, make the right decisions, and understand what actually mattered.

Eventually, we got help from someone who understood the process, and that changed everything.

Years later, after coaching, scouting, and recruiting at the college level, I saw the other side too.

I saw how coaches evaluate athletes.

I saw how fit matters.

I saw how timing, communication, and clarity can change the entire process.

That is why this page exists.

To help families feel more informed, more prepared, and less alone while navigating college recruiting.

If you’re new here, drop a 👋 in the comments.

If you have a recruiting question, drop it below too. I’ll try to answer as many as I can.

#CollegeRecruiting #SportsParents #StudentAthlete #RecruitingTips #PremierAthletes #RecruitNation

There are five legitimate ways to play college sports: NCAA D1, D2, D3, NAIA, and NJCAA. Each offers different scholarships, competition levels, time commitments, and college experiences.

Chasing the wrong level costs you time, money, and opportunities. Understanding the full landscape? That changes everything.

Comment MAP and I'll send you my free 12-question recruiting assessment — see exactly where your athlete stands and which path makes the most sense for your family.

#CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #CollegeSports #AthleticsScholarships #RecruitingProcess #RecruitingWithAlex #PremierAthletes

When a college coach calls — don’t panic.
Here’s what to say, and how to make a great impression 👇

As a former D1 coach who’s made hundreds of these calls, there’s nothing worse than losing momentum because a player isn’t ready.

Here’s what to expect (and how to prepare):

🎯 What they’ll ask you:
1️⃣ “How’s your season going?” → Start with the team, then your best stats.
2️⃣ “How are your grades?” → Know your GPA — keep them strong.
3️⃣ “Have you thought about a major?” → Show you’ve done your homework (“I’m thinking business, communication, or finance”).

🎯 What YOU should ask them:
1️⃣ “What’s a day in the life like for your players?”
2️⃣ “What are you looking for at my position?”
3️⃣ “How does your program focus on player development — athletically, in the weight room, and academically?”

These are the conversations that separate serious recruits from everyone else.

Comment MAP below and I’ll send you the 12-question quiz to see if your athlete’s mindset and habits match what college coaches look for.

#CollegeRecruiting #PremierAthletes #StudentAthlete #ParentsOfAthletes #AllSportsRecruiting #RecruitingTips #CollegeSports #CollegeAthleteJourney #HighSchoolAthlete #D1Recruiting #Whenacollegecoachcalls

History made in Granville.

Denison captured the first national championship in program history, turning a dream season into a championship legacy. A moment that will forever be part of Big Red baseball history.

#NCAAD3 #CollegeBaseball #DenisonBaseball #BigRed #NationalChampions

The standard remains the standard.

Tampa secured another national title and continued one of the most dominant runs in college baseball. Year after year, the Spartans prove why they're one of the premier programs in the country.

#NCAAD2 #CollegeBaseball #TampaBaseball #SpartansBaseball #NationalChampionshipBound

Florida State is one of those programs that gives families a lot to evaluate beyond the logo.

Strong academics.
ACC competition.
Championship history.
Big-time facilities.
Multiple dominant sports.
A true Power 4 college experience.

But here’s the important part:

A school can look great on paper and still need to be the right fit for your athlete.

When building a recruiting list, families should look at the full picture:

athletic level, academics, major, roster opportunity, location, campus life, and long-term development.

Save this if Florida State is on your athlete’s radar.
Share it with a family building their college list.

#FloridaState #CollegeRecruiting #StudentAthlete #SportsParents #RecruitingWithAlex

Most camp invites are NOT what parents think they are.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Just because your athlete got invited to a camp does NOT automatically mean they’re being heavily recruited.

A lot of these emails are mass blasts.

That’s why parents get frustrated…

They spend thousands on camps, hotels, flights, and showcases hoping for traction… and nothing happens.

Before committing to ANY camp, ask these 2 questions:

1️⃣ “Are you recruiting my position in my grad class?”
2️⃣ “Can you give feedback on my video?”

That second question is huge.

Because coaches who are genuinely interested usually engage.
Coaches who aren’t… usually don’t reply.

That alone can save families a ton of wasted time and money.

Then filter everything through:
✔️ Athletic fit
✔️ Academic fit
✔️ Geographic fit
✔️ Financial fit
✔️ Actual recruiting need

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t attending the MOST events…

They’re attending the RIGHT ones.

First-ever national title for Pearl River Community College softball. 

There is nothing like watching a program reach the top for the very first time.

This one meant everything and you could see it on every single face in that pile.

Comment ⭐ to celebrate this historic moment with them.

World Series champions. In just year two of the program.

Let that sink in. Northeast Alabama Community College did not wait for permission to compete at the highest level. They just went out and won it all.

Drop a 💧 in the comments if this gave you chills.

Most families are chasing logos…
Smart families are chasing FIT.

Because the “best” school for your athlete isn’t just about athletics. It’s about:

→ Academics
→ Campus environment
→ Opportunity
→ Development
→ Network
→ Student experience
→ And finding a place where they can actually thrive for 4 years.

As a former Division 1 coach, scout, and recruiter, I can tell you this:

Too many athletes waste years targeting schools that were never the right fit in the first place.

The families that win in recruiting usually aren’t the ones hoping to get discovered…

They’re the ones with a real strategy.

Georgia has some unbelievable universities that can open doors athletically AND academically if you know how to position yourself the right way.

Which school would your athlete choose? 👇

Comment the school name below and I’ll tell you:
• What level athlete typically gets recruited there
• What coaches look for
• And how realistic that fit is right now

Save this for later because choosing the right fit can change your athlete’s entire future.

A lot of families are doing all the things…

Tournaments.
Showcases.
Camps.
Emails.
Highlight videos.

But there’s still no traction.

Here’s why:

College coaches usually do not walk into events blindly hoping to discover players.

They already have names they want to see.

So if your athlete is not being positioned before the event, there’s a good chance they are just hoping to get noticed.

And hope is not a recruiting strategy.

The goal is to target the right schools, understand who actually needs your athlete’s position, and get in front of those coaches before they show up.

That’s how you flip the script.

You stop waiting to be recruited.

You start recruiting the schools.

If you want a better plan to help your athlete get seen by the right college coaches, comment NATION and I’ll send you the playbook.

A recruiting email should not be long.

It needs to be clear.

Coaches are busy, and they are usually scanning for the information that helps them evaluate quickly:

Position(s)
Grad year
Key metrics
Academic info
Video link
Upcoming schedule

The stronger the email, the easier it is for a coach to know whether they should keep watching, ask for more, or move on.

But here is the part families miss:

The email is only one piece.

A great email sent to the wrong school is still the wrong email.

A great email sent at the wrong time may still get buried.

A great email with no follow-up usually does not create momentum.

The bigger strategy is knowing who to contact, when to contact them, what to send, and how to follow up with purpose.

Save this before your athlete sends their next coach email.
Share it with a family trying to figure out coach communication.

#RecruitingTips #CollegeRecruiting #CoachCommunication #StudentAthlete #RecruitNation
